hero.title: Arts
hero.subtitle: An academic track focused on the study of human society, culture, behavior, and expression — exploring how humans interact, govern themselves, create, and think.

choiceInsights: Most students choose Arts for government jobs — about 30% are specifically targeting UPSC and state PSC exams.
25% are here by forced choice, not genuine preference — they couldn't get into Science and felt Arts was safer than Commerce.
The smallest group, just 5%, are genuinely pursuing creative expression. That's the number this stream's reputation suffers for.

myths.title: The "Dumping Ground" Fallacy
myths.myth: Arts is only for students who scored poorly in 10th grade.
myths.reality: The world's most influential policymakers, legal minds, and strategists actively choose Arts. It takes high intellectual maturity to analyze complex societal frameworks, human behavioral patterns, and political philosophies — it's a stream of choice for students targeting law, design, and governance.

myths.title: The "Zero Employability" Myth
myths.myth: If you take Arts, you will end up unemployed or in a low-paying job.
myths.reality: A generic Arts degree without direction is vulnerable. But specialized Humanities pathways — Law (CLAT/NLU), Design (NID/NIF), Clinical Psychology, and Policy — produce graduates who enter elite institutions with starting packages that match or exceed Science-stream averages.

myths.title: The AI Shift Reality
myths.myth: Arts skills are "soft" and therefore low-value in a tech economy.
myths.reality: AI and software now handle rote logic, basic coding, and standard accounting. What AI cannot do is navigate human nuance, cultural context, ethical ambiguity, and complex negotiations. The skills Arts trains — critical thinking, behavioral analysis, abstract problem-solving — are becoming the scarcest and highest-paid in the market.

modernCareers.name: Geopolitical Risk Consultant
modernCareers.techMultiplier: Big Data Risk Modeling + Trade Law

traditionalCareers.name: The Civil Servant (UPSC/IAS)
traditionalCareers.reality: The ultimate prestige trap. Millions spend years in brutal preparation for a handful of seats.

traditionalCareers.name: High School Teacher (Humanities)
traditionalCareers.reality: A stable but linearly capped career. Highly dependent on government hiring cycles.

traditionalCareers.name: Traditional Print / Local Journalist
traditionalCareers.reality: The old-media grind. Long hours, high stress, shrinking market due to digital disruption.

traditionalCareers.name: Local Litigation Lawyer
traditionalCareers.reality: Requires years of low-paid apprenticeship under senior advocates before any real income.

traditionalCareers.name: Govt. Clerk / SSC Employee
traditionalCareers.reality: The ultimate safe fallback — highly secure but the most linearly capped growth path of all.

conclusion: The Arts stream develops skills that are becoming increasingly valuable — communication, critical thinking, research, creativity, and understanding of human behavior. A stream alone does not decide a student's future. Success depends on awareness, skill development, consistency, and the ability to adapt to changing opportunities.
